text: The surgeon identifies the proximal and distal ends of the abnormal bowel segment. The mesentery is held perpendicular to the surgical instrument to create a mesenteric window at the site of bowel transection.
The surgeon uses a 2.5 mm linear stapler to divide the small bowel through the mesenteric window. The mesentery of the specimen is then divided using an electrosurgical device.

The surgeon places a traction suture at the anti-mesenteric side of the bowel to align the two portions before re-anastomosis, ensuring proper alignment and support at the end of the anastomotic staple line.
The surgeon uses hook electrocautery to create openings in the bowel lumen, taking care to avoid injury to the back wall of the bowel during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a 60mm long, 2.5mm linear stapler to reestablish intestinal continuity, ensuring the stapler is fully placed within the bowel lumen and the bowel is evenly aligned. For a larger anastomosis, the surgeon rotates the anastomosis 180 degrees to accommodate another stapler.
The surgeon inspects the stapled suture line for any signs of bleeding, applying interrupted sutures to control any bleeding observed. A proximal stitch is then placed to secure the anastomosis.

The surgeon closes the common enterotomy using a running stitch technique, employing a single layer closure with absorbable suture, such as a 2-0 polysorb. A target suture is placed to facilitate dexterity, minimizing tissue handling.
The surgeon utilizes sutures to ensure complete resection of the enterotomy, followed by a stapled closure performed in a Heinecke-Mikulitz orientation, closing the enterotomy transversally.

The completed suture line and staple line are visible. The surgeon places a stay suture at the end of the mesenteric defect and begins a running suture to close the remainder of the defect, finishing at the previously placed stay suture.
